tossups.question: On All Hallows Day, the protagonist of this novel cries while reading a prayer book a month after burying his two daughters, one of whom was learning Greek and was buried with her copybook. The protagonist of this novel comforts a superior by interpreting his dream of his seemingly upset older brother returning in a white, fiery light. This novel begins in the town of Putney as the young protagonist is beaten by his father (*) Walter, who says, “So now get up.” The protagonist raises Rafe Sadler at Austin Friars in this novel, which introduces the antagonistic Stephen Gardiner and the protagonist’s boss Cardinal Thomas Wolsey. For 10 points, name this novel followed by Bring Up the Bodies and The Mirror and the Light in a trilogy about Thomas Cromwell, written by Hilary Mantel.
tossups.question_sanitized: On All Hallows Day, the protagonist of this novel cries while reading a prayer book a month after burying his two daughters, one of whom was learning Greek and was buried with her copybook. The protagonist of this novel comforts a superior by interpreting his dream of his seemingly upset older brother returning in a white, fiery light. This novel begins in the town of Putney as the young protagonist is beaten by his father (*) Walter, who says, "So now get up." The protagonist raises Rafe Sadler at Austin Friars in this novel, which introduces the antagonistic Stephen Gardiner and the protagonist's boss Cardinal Thomas Wolsey. For 10 points, name this novel followed by Bring Up the Bodies and The Mirror and the Light in a trilogy about Thomas Cromwell, written by Hilary Mantel.

tossups.question: Part Two of this novel ends with Cicero’s story about how the poet Simonides invented the art of memory when he was able to identify all of the victims of the collapse of a banqueting hall. A character in this novel has a dream in which his dead brother Arthur appears to him bathed in white light, which he interprets as a bad omen. This novel opens with the protagonist being beaten up by his abusive father (*) Walter. This novel, whose fictionalized versions of historical personages include Rafe Sadler and Stephen Gardiner, is named for the seat of the Seymour family. This is the first novel in a trilogy that also includes Bring Up the Bodies and the as-yet-unpublished The Mirror and the Light. For 10 points, name this novel about Thomas Cromwell by Hilary Mantel.
tossups.question_sanitized: Part Two of this novel ends with Cicero's story about how the poet Simonides invented the art of memory when he was able to identify all of the victims of the collapse of a banqueting hall. A character in this novel has a dream in which his dead brother Arthur appears to him bathed in white light, which he interprets as a bad omen. This novel opens with the protagonist being beaten up by his abusive father (*) Walter. This novel, whose fictionalized versions of historical personages include Rafe Sadler and Stephen Gardiner, is named for the seat of the Seymour family. This is the first novel in a trilogy that also includes Bring Up the Bodies and the as-yet-unpublished The Mirror and the Light. For 10 points, name this novel about Thomas Cromwell by Hilary Mantel.

tossups.question: The protagonist of this novel recalls winning a bet to hold onto a snake for ten seconds, counted in German, despite it bit-ing him afer four. Multiple characters in this novel repeat the mantra “Arrange your face.” This novel’s protagonist fashions angel wings out of peacock feathers as a costume for his daughter to wear at a Christmas pageant. Because one character in this novel introduces himself by saying “Call me Risley,” everyone else jokingly refers to him by the nickname “Call-Me.” This novel begins with a gruesome scene where the main character lies barely conscious on the ground as his blacksmith father Wal-ter repeatedly kicks his head; as a result, its protagonist boards a ship for continental Europe. Stephen Gardiner antagonizes the protagonist of this novel, who rises from the right-hand man of Cardinal Wolsey to a position in which he negotiates the marriages of Anne Boleyn and Jane Seymour, whose family’s residence titles the novel. The author of this Booker-winning, 2009 novel won a second Booker in 2012 for its sequel Bringing Up the Bodies. For 10 points, name this novel about Thomas Cromwell, the frst in a series by Hilary Mantel.

packet.name: Round 14 - Maryland, Notre Dame, and Truman State

Because one character in this novel introduces himself by saying "Call me Risley," everyone else jokingly refers to him by the nickname "Call-Me." Stephen Gardiner antagonizes the protagonist of this novel, who rises from the right-hand man of Cardinal Wolsey to a position in which he negotiates the marriages of Anne Boleyn and Jane Seymour, whose family's residence titles the novel. The author of this Booker-winning, 2009 novel won a second Booker in 2012 for its sequel Bringing Up the Bodies. For 10 points, name this novel about Thomas Cromwell, the frst in a series by Hilary Mantel.

bonuses.leadin: The title curate magically keeps a whiskey bottle full during a weekend of drinking with the faithless priest Angwin in this author's novel Fludd. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The title curate magically keeps a whiskey bottle full during a weekend of drinking with the faithless priest Angwin in this author's novel Fludd. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author, who described her Catholic school upbringing in Giving Up the Ghost. This author fictionalized a man who states the world is not run "from the castle walls, but from counting houses."
Hilary Mantel broke with literary tradition by her positive depiction of Thomas Cromwell in a historical trilogy starting with this novel.
In contrast to Cromwell, Wolf Hall negatively depicted Thomas More, the author of this 1516 political allegory about an ideal society.
parts_sanitized: Name this author, who described her Catholic school upbringing in Giving Up the Ghost. This author fictionalized a man who states the world is not run "from the castle walls, but from counting houses."
Hilary Mantel broke with literary tradition by her positive depiction of Thomas Cromwell in a historical trilogy starting with this novel.
In contrast to Cromwell, Wolf Hall negatively depicted Thomas More, the author of this 1516 political allegory about an ideal society.
answers: Hilary Mantel [or Hilary Mary Mantel] (The quote is from Thomas Cromwell in a Mantel novel.)
answers_sanitized: Hilary Mantel [or Hilary Mary Mantel] (The quote is from Thomas Cromwell in a Mantel novel.)

bonuses.leadin: Though none of its action takes place at the title estate, this novel’s name symbolizes the danger its protagonist encounters. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 2009 Booker Prize winning novel about the rise of Thomas Cromwell whose sequel, Bring Up the Bodies, also won the prize in 2012.
Wolf Hall and Bring Up the Bodies were written by this author of historical fiction.
Wolf Hall ends with the death of this figure who is sympathetically portrayed in A Man for All Seasons. This author wrote Utopia.
answers_sanitized: Hilary Mary Mantel [or Hilary Mary Thompson]

Wolf Hall and Bring Up the Bodies were written by this author of historical fiction.

bonuses.leadin: In a play about this man, the Common Man appears as a steward, a jailor, a juror, and an executioner. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character who is killed at the end of Robert Bolt's play A Man for All Seasons. This real-life author wrote a book in which Raphael Hythlodaeus describes the communistic society on the title fictional island.
Thomas More's island has this name, derived from the Greek for "nowhere," which has since come to denote any imaginary perfect society.
In this 2009 novel by Hilary Mantel, Thomas More appears as a fanatic who accuses its protagonist, Thomas Cromwell, of supporting heretics. This novel's sequel is Bring Up the Bodies.

bonuses.leadin: Several small roles in this play are assumed by a sporadic narrator called the Common Man. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play by Robert Bolt, which shows the downfall and execution of Sir Thomas More during the reign of Henry VIII.

In A Man For All Seasons, More says he would give even this figure the benefit of law, rather than "cut down every law in England" to defeat him. Blake said that Milton was "of [this figure's] party without knowing it" while writing Paradise Lost.
This Booker-winning 2009 historical novel by Hilary Mantel, titled for the Seymour family estate, ends with Thomas More's beheading. Its 2012 sequel, Bring Up the Bodies, also won a Booker.
